alerts: Multiple failed login attempts|Login from unfamiliar geographic location|Impossible travel detection|Account lockout triggered|Anomalous authentication volume
clues: Password spraying pattern detected|Successful login after repeated failures|Privilege escalation attempt observed|Use of valid credentials from unusual IP range|Concurrent session anomalies detected
description: A real username and password has been obtained, guessed, bought, or found in a previous breach. The attacker walks in through the front door looking completely legitimate. No alarm, no broken window. Network defences are nearly useless here. Counter with MFA, Password Reset, Disable Account, and Revoke Tokens.
real_world: Verizon 2023 DBIR: credential abuse involved in over 80% of all breaches. Colonial Pipeline (2021): started with a single stolen VPN password on an account with no MFA.
hint: Strong counters: MFA, Password Reset, Disable Account, Revoke Tokens, Remote Access Suspension. Network-level defences are nearly useless - the attacker looks legitimate.

alerts: Suspicious process execution detected|Endpoint protection alert triggered|Unknown executable observed|System performance degradation|Unauthorized software installation detected
clues: Persistence mechanism identified|Command and control (C2) communication detected|Encoded payload execution observed|Malicious file hash matched|Privilege escalation via executable abuse
description: A harmful program is installed and running silently, watching keystrokes, copying files, or giving the attacker remote control. The longer it runs, the more it spreads. Stop it early with Application Whitelisting or Quarantine File. If delayed, Rebuild Endpoint is the only guaranteed fix.
real_world: NotPetya (2017): Russian military malware spread machine to machine causing $10B in damage to Maersk, Merck, and FedEx before most knew it was there.
hint: Strong counters: Application Whitelisting, Quarantine File, Host Isolation, Rebuild Endpoint. Speed matters - every turn it runs it spreads further.

alerts: Mass file modification detected|Endpoint encryption behavior observed|Backup system access anomaly|Sudden spike in disk activity|User unable to access files
clues: File encryption across multiple directories confirmed|Ransom note presence detected|Shadow copy deletion observed|Lateral encryption movement identified|Known ransomware family behavior matched
description: Every file is being encrypted. A ransom demand is on screen. Every turn you delay, more files lock and attackers destroy backups to maximise pressure. Speed is everything. Contain fast with Network Segmentation and Quarantine File. Survive long enough and Backup Recovery lets you recover without paying.
real_world: Colonial Pipeline (2021): paid $4.4M in Bitcoin after ransomware locked systems and caused fuel shortages across the entire US East Coast.
hint: Strong counters: Network Segmentation, Quarantine File, Host Isolation, Backup Recovery. Contain it fast - attackers actively destroy backups to remove your recovery options.

alerts: Unusual employee data access patterns|After-hours access detected|Excessive file downloads observed|Privilege misuse alert|Policy violation flagged
clues: Access to unrelated sensitive data confirmed|Large-scale internal data collection observed|Abnormal role-based access deviation|Unauthorized administrative actions detected|Repeated access to restricted resources
description: An employee, contractor, or partner is misusing legitimate access. Deliberate or accidental. You can't block what's already inside; network defences are useless. Investigate first, identify the source. Counter with Access Revocation, Least Privilege, and User Activity Monitoring.
real_world: Twitter hack (2022): attackers paid an employee to use internal admin tools. No exploit needed, just one person with keys. 130 high-profile accounts compromised.
hint: Strong counters: Access Revocation, Least Privilege, Disable Account, User Activity Monitoring. You cannot block what is already inside - investigate first, then act.

alerts: Unusual outbound traffic volume detected|Large data upload to cloud storage|USB device transfer activity detected|DLP (Data Loss Prevention) alert triggered|External file transfer detected
clues: Large compressed archive staged for transfer|Sensitive data transmitted outside network boundary|Use of encrypted outbound channels detected|Steganography or concealment techniques observed|Unusual DNS tunneling activity detected
description: Data is being quietly copied and sent out to an attacker's server or personal cloud. Patient, slow, nobody notices. Damage grows every turn and data can't be unsent. DLP Monitoring slows the leak. Block IP Address and Access Revocation are your strongest direct counters.
real_world: Target (2013): 40 million card numbers exfiltrated over three weeks through HVAC contractor credentials. Unusual outbound traffic was visible the whole time. Nobody looked.
hint: Strong counters: Remote Access Suspension, DLP Monitoring, Block IP Address, Access Revocation. Data cannot be unsent - stop the leak fast and contain what has already left.

alerts: Network traffic spike detected|Service availability degradation reported|Excessive connection requests observed|Bandwidth saturation alert|Firewall rate-limit triggered
clues: Distributed request sources identified|Botnet-like traffic behavior confirmed|SYN flood pattern detected|Application resource exhaustion observed|Repeated request amplification pattern identified
description: Fake traffic from thousands of sources is flooding the system so real users can't get through. The system isn't broken, just overwhelmed. Block IP Address and Emergency Firewall Rule are your strongest immediate counters. Web Application Firewall absorbs the flood. Use Service Restoration to get back online.
real_world: Mirai botnet (2016): 100,000 hijacked routers and cameras sent 1.2 terabits/sec at Dyn DNS. Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and Spotify all went offline.
hint: Strong counters: Block IP Address, Emergency Firewall Rule, Web Application Firewall, Service Restoration. The system is not broken - just overwhelmed. Contain the flood, then restore.

alerts: Suspicious email reported by user|Email security gateway alert triggered|Unexpected attachment detected|External sender spoofing warning|Credential submission page flagged
clues: Credential harvesting site identified|Email header spoofing confirmed|Malicious attachment execution observed|Social engineering lure confirmed|User credentials submitted to fake endpoint
description: A convincing fake email got someone to hand over their password. The attacker has real credentials and a foothold from one click. MFA and Security Awareness Training stop it before it lands. If someone already fell for it, Password Reset and Disable Account limit the damage.
real_world: DNC hack (2016): one spear-phishing email gave Russian military unit APT28 months of undetected access to emails and internal communications.
hint: Strong counters: MFA, Security Awareness Training, Password Reset, Disable Account. If someone already clicked, act fast - the attacker now has valid credentials.

alerts: Web application firewall alert triggered|HTTP request anomaly detected|Input validation failure logged|Database error exposure observed|Suspicious API activity detected
clues: SQL injection payload execution detected|Cross-site scripting (XSS) attempt identified|Authentication bypass technique observed|Directory traversal attack confirmed|Parameter tampering behavior detected
description: A weakness in the website or web tools is being actively exploited: SQL injection, session hijacking, or login bypass. A Web Application Firewall blocks many automatically. Patch Management and Secure Configuration close the vulnerabilities. If you can't patch, Application Shutdown buys time.
real_world: Equifax (2017): 147 million Americans' data stolen through an unpatched Apache Struts vulnerability. The patch had been available for months. Nobody applied it.
hint: Strong counters: Web Application Firewall, Patch Management, Secure Configuration, Application Shutdown. If patching is not immediately possible, take the application offline until it is.
